text: This study offers the first comparative analysis of the political, military and economic aspects of the Soviet occupation of Austria, Hungary and Romania. Based on unique documentation from national archives in all three countries as well as Russia, the authors contest the prevailing interpretation of Soviet behaviour during this period. While existing research has concentrated overwhelmingly on the political aspect of the occupation (i.e. Stalin's decision to impose the Bolshevik system in each country), this book demonstrates that, in addition to political considerations, imperial motives of a military and economic character played a decisive role in the Soviet conquest of Eastern-Central Europe. The occupation and its impact on the local population is also considered in unprecedented depth. This includes descriptions of war crimes, theft and looting, forced deportations, and sexual crimes committed during the period.
